epigraph.text: Though it is the smallest of all seeds, yet when it grows, it is the largest of garden plants and becomes a tree.
epigraph.quote: The concept of limit is the foundation of the higher calculus.

content.text:
In Section [sec:tangent-velocity] we repeatedly encountered expressions 
like $\limtozero{h}\,(2+h)$ and declared, without formal justification, that 
such a quantity "approaches" a particular value. We computed secant slopes 
for smaller and smaller values of $h$, watched the numbers converge, and 
trusted the pattern. But mathematics demands more than trust in patterns. 
In this section we give the concept of a limit its proper name and explore 
what it means for a function to approach a value—even at points where the 
function itself may not be defined.
An Intuitive Definition of Limits

Consider the function
\[
 f(x) = \frac{x^{2} - 1}{x - 1}.
\]
This function is undefined at $x = 1$ because substitution yields 
$\frac{0}{0}$. But what happens to $f(x)$ as $x$ gets *close to* $1$ 
without actually equaling $1$? Let us investigate with a table.

The values of $f(x)$ get closer and closer to $2$ as $x$ approaches $1$ from 
either side. We can verify this algebraically: for $x \neq 1$,
\[
 f(x) = \frac{x^{2}-1}{x-1} = \frac{(x-1)(x+1)}{x-1} = x + 1.
\]
So $f$ agrees with the function $g(x) = x + 1$ everywhere except at $x = 1$, 
where $f$ is undefined and $g(1) = 2$. The graph of $f$ is the line $y = x+1$ 
with a hole at the point $(1, 2)$.

We write
\[
 \limto{x \to 1} \frac{x^{2}-1}{x-1} = 2,
\]
and we say "the limit of $f(x)$ as $x$ approaches $1$ is $2$."

content.title: Limit of a Function (Intuitive)
content.content:
We write
\[
 \limto{x \to a} f(x) = L
\]
and say "the **limit** of $f(x)$, as $x$ 
approaches $a$, equals $L$" if we can make the values of $f(x)$ 
*arbitrarily close* to $L$ by restricting $x$ to be *sufficiently 
close* to $a$ (on either side) but not equal to $a$.

content.title: The limit does not care about $f(a)$
content.content:
The value of $\limto{x \to a} f(x)$ depends only on the values of $f$ 
*near* $a$, not on the value of $f$ *at* $a$ (which may not even 
exist). In the example above, $f(1)$ is undefined, yet 
$\limto{x \to 1} f(x) = 2$. Even if $f$ were defined at $a$ with a 
"wrong" value—say $f(1) = 17$—the limit as $x \to 1$ would 
*still* be $2$.

content.text:
This connects directly to Section [sec:tangent-velocity]. When we wrote 
$\limtozero{h}\,(2+h) = 2$, we were computing a limit: the values of $2+h$ 
approach $2$ as $h$ approaches $0$. The limit gave meaning to a difference 
quotient that was undefined at $h = 0$.
Estimating Limits Numerically and Graphically

content.title: Estimating a limit from a table
content.problem:
Estimate $\displaystyle\limtozero{x}\,\frac{\sin x}{x}$ using a table of 
values. (*Use radian mode.*)

content.title: Estimating a limit from a graph
content.problem:
Let $f$ be defined by
\[
 f(x) = \begin{cases}
 x + 1 & \text{if } x < 2, \\
 5 & \text{if } x = 2, \\
 7 - x & \text{if } x > 2.
 \end{cases}
\]
Find $\limto{x \to 2} f(x)$.
content.solution:
As $x$ approaches $2$ from the left, $f(x) = x+1$ approaches $2+1=3$. As 
$x$ approaches $2$ from the right, $f(x) = 7-x$ approaches $7-2=5$.

The left-hand approach gives $3$ and the right-hand approach gives $5$. 
Since these are different, the limit *does not exist*.

Note that $f(2) = 5$, but this is irrelevant to the limit question. The 
function value at the point does not determine the limit.

content.text: One-Sided Limits

The previous example reveals that the behavior of a function as $x$ 
approaches $a$ may differ depending on whether $x$ approaches from the left 
or from the right. This motivates the notion of *one-sided limits*.

content.title: One-Sided Limits
content.content:
We write
\[
 \limto{x \to a^{-}} f(x) = L
\]
and say "the **left-hand limit** of $f(x)$ as 
$x$ approaches $a$ is $L$" if $f(x)$ can be made arbitrarily close to $L$ 
by taking $x$ sufficiently close to $a$ with $x < a$.

Similarly,
\[
 \limto{x \to a^{+}} f(x) = L
\]
is the **right-hand limit**, requiring $x > a$.

content.text:
In Example [ex:limit-graph-piecewise], we found 
$\limto{x \to 2^{-}} f(x) = 3$ and $\limto{x \to 2^{+}} f(x) = 5$.
The following theorem connects one-sided limits to the two-sided limit.

content.title: Existence of a Two-Sided Limit
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
In other words, the two-sided limit exists and equals $L$ precisely when 
both one-sided limits exist and agree on the same value. When the one-sided 
limits disagree—as in Example [ex:limit-graph-piecewise]—the 
two-sided limit does not exist.

content.title: The Heaviside step function
content.problem:
The **Heaviside function** is defined by
\[
 H(t) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } t < 0, \\ 1 & \text{if } t \geq 0. \end{cases}
\]
Evaluate $\limto{t \to 0^{-}} H(t)$, \;$\limto{t \to 0^{+}} H(t)$, \;and 
$\limto{t \to 0} H(t)$.
content.solution:
For $t < 0$ (approaching from the left), $H(t) = 0$, so 
$\limto{t \to 0^{-}} H(t) = 0$.

For $t > 0$ (approaching from the right), $H(t) = 1$, so 
$\limto{t \to 0^{+}} H(t) = 1$.

Since $0 \neq 1$, the one-sided limits disagree, and by 
Theorem [thm:two-sided], $\limto{t \to 0} H(t)$ does not exist.

The Heaviside function models an abrupt switch—a light turning on, an 
electrical circuit closing. The "jump" at $t=0$ is precisely what 
prevents the two-sided limit from existing.

content.text: When Limits Fail to Exist

content.title: Zeno of Elea (c.\,490--430 BC)
content.content:
The Greek philosopher Zeno devised a series of famous paradoxes about 
motion. In the paradox of Achilles and the Tortoise, swift Achilles can 
never overtake the slow tortoise because he must first reach the point 
where the tortoise was, by which time the tortoise has moved ahead. The 
resolution of Zeno's paradoxes requires the concept of a limit—in 
particular, the fact that an infinite sum can have a *finite* value. 
Zeno's arguments troubled mathematicians and philosophers for over two 
millennia, until the rigorous theory of limits, developed by Cauchy 
and Weierstrass in the 19th century, laid them to rest.

content.text: Not every limit exists. There are three common ways a limit can fail.

*1. Different one-sided limits (a jump).** 
We saw this with the Heaviside function and the piecewise function in 
Example [ex:limit-graph-piecewise].

*2. Unbounded behavior.** 
Consider $f(x) = 1/x^{2}$ near $x=0$. As $x \to 0$, the values of $f(x)$ 
grow without bound: $f(0.1) = 100$, $f(0.01) = 10{,}000$, 
$f(0.001) = 1{,}000{,}000$, and so on. The function does not approach any 
finite number $L$, so $\limtozero{x}\,\frac{1}{x^{2}}$ does not exist 
(as a finite limit). We sometimes write 
$\limtozero{x}\,\frac{1}{x^{2}} = \infty$ to indicate the unbounded growth, 
but this is *not* saying the limit exists—$\infty$ is not a real 
number. We study these *infinite limits* in detail in 
Section [sec:infinite-limits].

*3. Oscillation.** 
Consider $g(x) = \sin(1/x)$ near $x = 0$. As $x \to 0$, the argument 
$1/x$ grows without bound, causing $\sin(1/x)$ to oscillate between $-1$ 
and $1$ infinitely often. The function never settles toward any single 
value, so $\limtozero{x}\,\sin(1/x)$ does not exist.

content.title: A limit involving $1/x$
content.solution:
As $x \to 0^{+}$ (positive values approaching zero), $1/x$ grows 
without bound: $1/0.1 = 10$, $1/0.01 = 100$, $1/0.001 = 1000$, etc.

As $x \to 0^{-}$ (negative values approaching zero), $1/x$ decreases 
without bound: $1/(-0.1) = -10$, $1/(-0.01) = -100$, etc.

The one-sided limits diverge in opposite directions:
\[
 \limto{x \to 0^{+}} \frac{1}{x} = +\infty, \qquad
 \limto{x \to 0^{-}} \frac{1}{x} = -\infty.
\]
Therefore $\limtozero{x}\,\dfrac{1}{x}$ does not exist (even as an 
infinite limit, since the two sides disagree).

content.title: Estimating Limits Numerically
content.content:
To estimate $\limto{x \to a} f(x)$ using a table of values:

1. Choose values of $x$ approaching $a$ from the left: 
 $a - 0.1$, $a - 0.01$, $a - 0.001$, …

2. Choose values approaching from the right: 
 $a + 0.1$, $a + 0.01$, $a + 0.001$, …

3. Compute $f(x)$ for each.

4. If both columns approach the same value $L$, estimate the limit 
 as $L$. If they approach different values (or grow unbounded, or 
 oscillate), the two-sided limit does not exist.

content.text: More Examples

content.title: A limit that exists despite a hole
content.solution:
Direct substitution gives $\frac{0}{0}$, which is indeterminate. We factor:
\[
 \frac{x^{2}-9}{x-3} = \frac{(x-3)(x+3)}{x-3} = x + 3
 \qquad (x \neq 3).
\]
Since $f(x) = x+3$ for all $x \neq 3$, and we only care about values 
*near* $3$ (not at $3$):
\[
 \limto{x \to 3}\,\frac{x^{2}-9}{x-3} = \limto{x \to 3}(x+3) = 6.
\]

content.title: A limit involving absolute value
content.solution:
Recall that $\abs{x} = x$ when $x > 0$ and $\abs{x} = -x$ when $x < 0$.

**From the right** ($x > 0$): \;
$\dfrac{\abs{x}}{x} = \dfrac{x}{x} = 1$, \;so 
$\limto{x \to 0^{+}}\dfrac{\abs{x}}{x} = 1$.

**From the left** ($x < 0$): \;
$\dfrac{\abs{x}}{x} = \dfrac{-x}{x} = -1$, \;so 
$\limto{x \to 0^{-}}\dfrac{\abs{x}}{x} = -1$.

Since $1 \neq -1$, the two-sided limit does not exist.

content.title: One-sided limits of a piecewise function
content.problem:
Let
\[
 g(x) = \begin{cases}
 x^{2} & \text{if } x < 1, \\
 2 & \text{if } x = 1, \\
 3 - x & \text{if } x > 1.
 \end{cases}
\]
Find $\limto{x \to 1^{-}} g(x)$, \;$\limto{x \to 1^{+}} g(x)$, \;and 
$\limto{x \to 1} g(x)$.
content.solution:
**Left-hand limit:** As $x \to 1^{-}$, we use the rule $g(x) = x^{2}$, 
so $\limto{x \to 1^{-}} g(x) = 1^{2} = 1$.

**Right-hand limit:** As $x \to 1^{+}$, we use $g(x) = 3-x$, so 
$\limto{x \to 1^{+}} g(x) = 3 - 1 = 2$.

Since $1 \neq 2$, the two-sided limit $\limto{x \to 1} g(x)$ does not exist.

Again, the value $g(1) = 2$ plays no role in the limit computation.

content.title: A limit where one-sided limits agree
content.problem:
Let
\[
 f(x) = \begin{cases}
 x^{2} + 1 & \text{if } x \leq 2, \\
 4x - 3 & \text{if } x > 2.
 \end{cases}
\]
Evaluate $\limto{x \to 2} f(x)$.
content.solution:
**Left-hand limit:** $\limto{x \to 2^{-}} f(x) = 2^{2}+1 = 5$.

**Right-hand limit:** $\limto{x \to 2^{+}} f(x) = 4(2)-3 = 5$.

Both one-sided limits equal $5$, so by Theorem [thm:two-sided]:
\[
 \limto{x \to 2} f(x) = 5.
\]
Moreover, $f(2) = 2^{2}+1 = 5$ also equals $5$, so in this case the 
function value and the limit happen to agree. (They need not—but when 
they do, we will later call $f$ *continuous* at $x=2$.)

content.text: Infinite Limits: A First Look

Although we will study infinite limits thoroughly in 
Section [sec:infinite-limits], it is worth noting the notation here.

When the values of $f(x)$ grow without bound as $x \to a$, we write 
$\limto{x \to a} f(x) = \infty$ (or $-\infty$). This is a descriptive 
shorthand, not a claim that the limit "equals infinity." The symbol 
$\infty$ is not a real number; the limit does not exist in the usual sense. 
We say $f$ has a **vertical asymptote** at 
$x = a$ in this situation.

content.title: Vertical asymptote
content.solution:
As $x \to 3$ from either side, $(x-3)^{2}$ is a small *positive* 
number, so $\frac{1}{(x-3)^{2}}$ is a large positive number. We write
\[
 \limto{x \to 3}\,\frac{1}{(x-3)^{2}} = \infty.
\]
The graph has a vertical asymptote at $x = 3$. Since both one-sided limits 
tend to $+\infty$, we can write the two-sided statement.

Compare with $g(x) = \frac{1}{x-3}$, where 
$\limto{x \to 3^{+}} g(x) = +\infty$ and 
$\limto{x \to 3^{-}} g(x) = -\infty$. Here the two sides disagree 
(in sign), so we would simply say $\limto{x \to 3} g(x)$ does not exist.

content.title: Augustin-Louis Cauchy (1789--1857)
content.content:
It was the French mathematician Cauchy who first gave a clear, rigorous 
definition of the limit in his 1821 textbook *Cours d'analyse*. 
Before Cauchy, calculus had been practiced successfully for over 150 years 
using intuitive (and sometimes contradictory) notions of infinitesimals. 
Cauchy insisted on logical precision: a quantity "has the limit $L$" if 
the difference between it and $L$ can be made "as small as one wishes." 
His student Karl Weierstrass later refined this into the formal 
$\varepsilon$-$\delta$ definition we study in 
Section [sec:epsilon-delta]. Cauchy, a devout Catholic, saw no conflict 
between faith and rigorous mathematics. He wrote: "I am a Christian; 
that is to say, I believe in the divinity of Jesus Christ, as did Tycho 
Brahe, Copernicus, Descartes, Newton, Fermat, Leibniz, Pascal…"

content.caption: A removable discontinuity: the limit exists but the function is undefined.
content.alt: A coordinate plane showing the function f of x equals x squared minus 1, all divided by x minus 1. This simplifies to x plus 1 for all x not equal to 1. The graph is a straight line with an open circle (hole) at the point (1, 2), indicating the function is undefined there. Dashed lines extend from the hole to both axes. Green arrows approach the hole from both sides, showing that the limit as x approaches 1 equals 2.

content.caption: A piecewise function with different one-sided limits at $x = 2$.
content.alt: A coordinate plane showing a piecewise function. For x less than 2, the function follows one curve approaching a value L minus. For x greater than or equal to 2, the function follows a different curve starting from a value L plus. Open and closed circles at x equals 2 show the jump discontinuity. The left and right limits are different.

content.caption: One-sided limits exist but differ, so the two-sided limit does not exist.
content.alt: A coordinate plane showing a function with a jump discontinuity at x equals a. The left branch approaches the value L1 from below as x approaches a from the left. The right branch approaches a different value L2 from above as x approaches a from the right. Open circles mark both limits at x equals a. Since L1 does not equal L2, the two-sided limit does not exist.

content.caption: The Heaviside step function has a jump discontinuity at $t = 0$.
content.alt: A coordinate plane showing the Heaviside step function H of t. For t less than zero, the function equals zero, shown as a horizontal line on the x-axis. For t greater than or equal to zero, the function equals one, shown as a horizontal line at height one. At t equals zero, there is an open circle at (0, 0) and a closed circle at (0, 1), indicating a jump discontinuity.

content.caption: The function $\frac{1}{x^2}$ grows without bound as $x \to 0$.
content.alt: A coordinate plane showing f of x equals 1 over x squared. The function approaches positive infinity from both sides as x approaches zero, creating a vertical asymptote at x equals zero. The curve is symmetric about the y-axis, with both branches shooting upward near the origin and approaching zero as x goes to plus or minus infinity.

content.caption: The function $\sin(1/x)$ oscillates infinitely as $x \to 0$.
content.alt: A coordinate plane showing f of x equals sine of 1 over x. As x approaches zero, the function oscillates infinitely many times between negative 1 and positive 1, with the oscillations becoming increasingly rapid. The limit does not exist because the function never settles to a single value.

content.caption: The function $1/x$ approaches $+\infty$ from the right and $-\infty$ from the left.
content.alt: A coordinate plane showing f of x equals 1 over x. The function has a vertical asymptote at x equals zero. As x approaches zero from the right, the function goes to positive infinity. As x approaches zero from the left, the function goes to negative infinity. The two branches are in opposite quadrants, creating a hyperbola shape.

marginNotes.text:
Now faith is confidence in what we hope for 
and assurance about what we do not see.

marginNotes.author: Augustin-Louis Cauchy
marginNotes.text:
One must give the most 
precise definition to the word {limit

marginNotes.author: Blaise Pascal
marginNotes.text:
The heart has its reasons, which 
reason does not know.
